course_title: Global Hotel, Resort and Casino Management

learning_outcomes.description: Identify and discuss the concepts and theories in global hotel, resort and casino management.
Evaluate the impacts of social, economical and cultural factors on global hotel, resort and casino management.
Assess managerial problems within hotel, resort and casino industries.
Apply research skills and techniques collaboratively to solve managerial problems and develop solutions for industry practitioners.

assessments.task_description:
This assignment requires your team to produce a 20-minute PowerPoint video presentation that critically examines the viability of Queen's Wharf Brisbane as an integrated resort, with particular attention to the role and function of the casino within the precinct. In your presentation, your team must address the following project question: 

“To what extent is the casino expected to contribute to the future viability of Queen's Wharf Brisbane under contemporary regulatory, social, and market conditions?”

Queen’s Wharf Brisbane is a large-scale integrated resort precinct in Queensland that includes a major casino component. The casino replaced the former Treasury Casino when the new resort opened in August 2024 and forms a central part of the precinct’s hospitality and tourism offering. In recent years, Star Entertainment Group has faced increased financial and regulatory pressures, including heightened scrutiny of governance and compliance practices and the need to restructure its ownership and financing arrangements. As part of this process, Star has agreed to sell its 50 per cent equity stake in Queen’s Wharf Brisbane to its Hong Kong–based partners, resulting in changes to the ownership structure and operational arrangements of the casino.

In addressing the project question, your presentation should:

  • Conceptualise what “future viability” means for an integrated resort, clearly explaining the criteria you use to assess viability over time (e.g. economic sustainability, regulatory feasibility, social acceptability, market resilience).
  • Analyse the expected role of the casino in contributing to the future performance of Queen’s Wharf Brisbane, including how it is anticipated to interact with non-gaming components such as hotels, food and beverage, and entertainment.
  • Evaluate contemporary regulatory conditions affecting casino operations in Queensland and discuss how these conditions may shape the casino’s future capacity to contribute to the integrated resort.
  • Examine social and cultural conditions and expectations, including evolving public attitudes toward gambling and how these may influence the casino’s social licence to operate in the future.
  • Assess current and emerging market conditions, considering trends in tourism demand, competitive dynamics, and the relative importance of the casino compared with alternative attractions over time.
  • Synthesise insights across regulatory, social, and market dimensions to form an evidence-based judgement on the extent to which the casino is expected to contribute to the future viability of Queen’s Wharf Brisbane, acknowledging uncertainty where appropriate.

FORMAT:

  • This assignment requires your team to create a recorded PowerPoint slideshow (maximum 20 minutes) with video on, as if presenting to a live audience. If you are unfamiliar with recording a PowerPoint slideshow, please click HERE for guidance.
  • All team members must appear in the video presentation. When presenting, each member is required to record both their physical appearance and voice (narration).
  • Each team needs to submit one (1) MS PowerPoint file to the course Blackboard site. A team representative should be appointed to submit the file on behalf of all members. Only one submission per team is required; individual submissions are not necessary.
  • The MS PowerPoint file format (PPTX) is strongly recommended for the video presentation. If alternative software is used, it is the team's responsibility to ensure successful submission and accessibility for the teaching team without requiring additional software. Uploading a video file (e.g., MP4) directly to Blackboard is likely to result in submission failure.
  • Please note that the file submission will take time, particularly when submitting close to the deadline. Without approved extension, any file submission after the due date will incur a late submission penalty. Plan your submission accordingly.
  • In some cases, large PPTX files may not be playable on the web. This is acceptable. The teaching team will download and review submitted files offline. As long as your submission record is visible in Blackboard, your submission will be considered valid. Please ensure that all your team members retain the submission record.
  • The teaching team will mark the file submitted to the system, whether it is an incomplete or incorrect file.

PEER EVALUATION

A peer evaluation is required for this team assignment. Individual marks will be moderated based on each student's contribution to the assessment task, as indicated by peer ratings submitted through BuddyCheck. Final individual grades will be calculated by weighting the team mark according to the peer evaluation scores.

AI Statement:

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Please submit a presentation file and an associated file, if any, to Blackboard.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
This individual assignment requires you to prepare a report of up to 3,000 words that critically examines service robots and artificial intelligence (AI) and evaluates their applicability within the hospitality industry.

The hospitality industry is increasingly shaped by advances in automation, AI, and service robotics. While service robots have already been introduced in selected hospitality contexts, their use raises fundamental questions regarding the nature of hospitality and the role of technology in service delivery. This assignment requires you to critically examine these issues by drawing on established theory, empirical research, and documented industry practice, rather than speculative or anecdotal claims.

In this report, you are expected to:

  • Examine what constitutes hospitality, drawing on established academic literature and industry frameworks to identify core characteristics and principles of hospitality.
  • Explain service robots and AI in the hospitality context, including what they are and how they function, using clear and accessible language suitable for a non-technical audience.
  • Critically evaluate the extent to which service delivered by service robots and AI can be considered hospitality, using empirical studies, theoretical arguments, and documented hospitality applications to support your discussion.
  • Drawing on the evidence and arguments established in the preceding sections, assess how hospitality service delivery is likely to evolve over the next 10 years, with particular attention to the complementary or substitutive roles of service robots, AI and human employees. The assessment must be grounded in current research trends, industry reports, and observed adoption patterns, rather than unsupported speculation.

FORMAT

  • This is a free-style report with no fixed structure to follow. However, a clear introduction - body - conclusion structure is recommended.
  • A succinct report is expected.

GUIDELINES FOR REPORT PREPARATION

  • You are encouraged to spend enough time researching the conceptualisation of hospitality, service robots, and AI. A clear and accurate understanding of these concepts is essential before evaluating their applicability within the hospitality industry.
  • Your discussion must be grounded in academic literature and supported by relevant, real-world examples.
  • In explaining key terms and concepts, you must not copy and paste from existing sources. All explanations should be rephrased and presented in your own words, using layman's terms where appropriate.

Oral Check-in (individual)

To ensure academic integrity, an oral check-in has been implemented for this assessment task. The oral check-in will be scheduled in Week 13 after the submission due date, with five minutes allocated per student. If a submission is made after the due date, the oral check-in of the individual will be scheduled at a later time (e.g., during the exam block). During the oral check-in, you will be required to provide verbal responses to randomly selected questions related to your report and, where necessary, your team presentation.

The oral check-in is assessed on a pass/fail basis against specific criteria of the assessment rubric, totally 60% of the assessment. Students who fail the oral check-in will receive a 0 for these criteria. A fail includes cases where no attempt is made to complete the oral check-in. 

Please Note: The assessment will be recorded for marking purposes per UQ Policy.

AI Statement:

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
